card.title: How Ocean Currents Control the Climate
card.teaser: A slow-motion river in the Atlantic keeps London warmer than Labrador — and it's starting to falter.
card.coverImagePrompt: A satellite-style view of the North Atlantic with swirling blue and red temperature gradients tracing the Gulf Stream from Florida to Iceland, scientific visualisation aesthetic, deep cinematic blues

lessons.title: Two cities, same latitude
lessons.body: London sits at 51° north. So does the southern tip of Labrador, Canada. Labrador winters plunge below minus 20°C. London flirts with freezing. The difference isn't magic — it's water. A vast, invisible ocean conveyor carries tropical heat northeast across the Atlantic and exhales it over Europe. Without it, the British Isles would feel a lot more like Hudson Bay.

visual.imagePrompt: Two cities side by side on a map at the same latitude — one snow-covered Labrador village, one mild green London scene, split-frame

lessons.title: The thermohaline conveyor
lessons.body: Ocean currents are driven by two engines: wind and density. The 'thermohaline' circulation — from 'thermo' (heat) and 'haline' (salt) — runs on the second. When warm salty water reaches the North Atlantic, it cools and sinks, pulling more warm water up behind it like a siphon. That descent feeds a deep, cold river that winds around the globe for roughly 1,000 years before resurfacing.

keyTerms.definition: The Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation, the engine of North Atlantic heat transport
Circulation driven by differences in temperature and salt content

visual.imagePrompt: A cutaway diagram of the global ocean conveyor belt, warm red surface currents moving north, cold blue deep currents returning

lessons.title: Heat, food, and weather
lessons.body: Currents don't just move warmth. They move nutrients, oxygen, and eventually rainfall. Upwelling along the coast of Peru lifts cold nutrient-rich water to the surface, sustaining one of the world's richest fisheries. Shifts in that same current — an El Niño year — can crash the anchovy harvest, change monsoon patterns in India, and flood California within months.

visual.imagePrompt: A school of silver anchovies swarming near a Peruvian coastline, turquoise water, sunbeams breaking through the surface

lessons.title: The conveyor is slowing
lessons.body: As Arctic ice melts, freshwater floods the North Atlantic, making the water less dense and harder to sink. Measurements published in Nature in 2018 show the AMOC has weakened about 15% since the mid-20th century — the slowest it has been in at least a millennium. A 2023 study in Nature Communications warned of a potential collapse sometime between 2025 and 2095 if emissions stay high.
lessons.funFact: The 2004 film 'The Day After Tomorrow' badly exaggerated the timing, but the underlying worry — a stalled AMOC triggering regional cooling in Europe — is taken seriously by oceanographers.

visual.imagePrompt: A stark scientific graph on dark background showing AMOC strength declining across decades, annotated with an arrow toward a 'tipping point'

lessons.title: A slow lever on everything
lessons.body: Currents are easy to ignore because they're invisible and slow. But they redistribute more heat than every weather system combined, and they respond to things we're doing now — melting ice, warming surfaces, changing salinity. A climate without its currents wouldn't be our climate at all.

quiz.question: What does AMOC stand for?
quiz.explanation: The Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation is the system that carries tropical heat northeast and returns cold deep water southward.

quiz.question: What drives the 'thermohaline' part of ocean circulation?
quiz.explanation: Thermohaline means heat plus salt — denser cold salty water sinks, pulling warmer water behind it.

quiz.question: Why does melting Arctic ice threaten the AMOC?
options: It cools the tropical oceans too quickly
It dumps freshwater that makes the North Atlantic less dense
It blocks shipping routes that influence wind
It raises the salinity of the Pacific
quiz.explanation: Freshwater is less dense than salt water, so added meltwater resists sinking — which is the motor of the overturning circulation.

sources.title: Observed fingerprint of a weakening Atlantic Ocean overturning circulation
Warning of a forthcoming collapse of the Atlantic meridional overturning circulation
